student_message: Um, yes, I can try. I read it three times to be sure. Sorry. Did I mess up already?
final_message:
Math can be hard sometimes, so it is totally okay to feel nervous! You haven't messed up at all. In fact, you did a great job reading it three times to be sure. Remember, making mistakes is just a normal part of learning and how our brains grow, and I am right here with you! 💛 

Let's gently start looking at reflection symmetry together. A line of symmetry is just a special fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a fold line that goes straight up and down, and some go side to side. Think about the letter $$A$$. If you draw a line right down the middle, both sides match perfectly like a mirror! Can you picture that fold line in your mind?

orchestrator_intent.goal: Reassure the student they haven't messed up and gently begin discussing reflection symmetry.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The student is fearful and anxious about making mistakes. Encouragement is needed to build confidence before continuing the lesson.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.
